metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add a concrete proving command for AC13 (e.g. a grep that asserts absence of known anti-patterns like 'do not', 'avoid', 'never' appearing more than N times per section, or a linter rule) OR explicitly document that this is a mandatory human-review gate with a named checklist artifact (e.g. a reviewer sign-off comment in the PR), so the AC is evaluable and evidenced.
findings.finding: AC13 (style check) names no deterministic proving command and is framed as a manual human review ('reviewed section-by-section against the pink-elephant rule stated above'). There is no repo artifact, CI check, or deterministic command that produces evidence on success, making it neither in-session-evaluable by an automated agent nor falsifiable by CI.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite AC7's second proving command to unambiguously handle both the 'qualified' and 'rephrased' outcomes, e.g. assert the test-design.md citation appears in the same region AND that the old standalone phrasing is absent: `awk '/Phase 2/,/Phase 3/' .../llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md | grep -q 'test-design.md'` (positive) paired with `! grep -q 'a single failing eval/behavioural assertion' .../llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` (negative) so each result is unambiguous.
findings.finding: AC7's proving command for llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md is ambiguous: the second grep (`grep -n 'an eval/behavioural assertion that fails'`) produces zero results if the sentence is *rephrased* and nonzero results if it is *qualified*, but the plan says both outcomes count as passing ('qualified or rephrased'). A zero-result grep conventionally signals failure in a shell pipeline, yet 'rephrased' should also pass — so the command either gives a false-negative when the sentence is correctly removed/reworded, or requires an executor to interpret zero results as a success signal, which is non-obvious and error-prone.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the second grep with two commands: (1) a positive assertion that `test-design.md` appears in the Phase 2 section (already in the first grep, scoped correctly), and (2) a negative assertion `! grep -q 'an eval/behavioural assertion that fails' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` so a passing result unambiguously means the old single-sample language was removed or reworded — zero results = pass for the negative assertion, which is conventional.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a verify step to AC bullet 14 (or a new bullet) that confirms `make test` collects and runs `tests/unit/test_skill_shared_sync.py` — e.g. `make test 2>&1 | grep -q test_skill_shared_sync` or an equivalent that proves the test is wired into the gating CI command.
findings.finding: The plan body declares a key constraint — 'runs under `make test`, an existing gating CI command, mirroring the repo's regenerate-and-diff drift-gate convention' — but AC bullet 14 (the only CI-gate verification) checks only `make lint` and `.venv/bin/pytest tests/unit/test_skill_shared_sync.py -q`, never `make test`. An implementer who drops the new test file in a location not picked up by `make test` satisfies every AC while violating the stated gating constraint.
findings.location: Plan body ('it runs under `make test`, an existing gating CI command') vs AC bullet 14 (`make lint` and `.venv/bin/pytest ... -q`)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC bullet 14 to: "`make lint`, `make test` (confirm `tests/unit/test_skill_shared_sync.py` is collected), and `.venv/bin/pytest tests/unit/test_skill_shared_sync.py -q` all pass."
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's claim that the new pytest drift test 'mirrors the repo's regenerate-and-diff drift-gate convention (prompt-index, public-types, env-vars)' — those gates are CI shell steps in test.yml, not pytest tests; clarify that the new test is a pytest unit test (a different but valid mechanism) rather than claiming it mirrors the shell-script pattern.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the new pytest drift test 'mirrors the repo's regenerate-and-diff drift-gate convention (prompt-index, public-types, env-vars)', but probing the repo shows the actual prompt-index, public-types, and env-vars drift gates all run as CI shell steps in .github/workflows/test.yml — NOT as pytest unit tests under 'make test'. The plan's new test runs under 'make test' (pytest), which is a distinct mechanism. If the implementer patterns the new gate after the existing drift-gate mechanism (shell steps in test.yml), they will place the test in the wrong location; if they follow the plan's 'make test' instruction, the gating mechanism differs from what the plan describes as its model.
findings.location: Plan body, Reference mechanism section: 'runs under `make test`, an existing gating CI command, mirroring the repo's regenerate-and-diff drift-gate convention (prompt-index, public-types, env-vars)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'mirroring the repo's regenerate-and-diff drift-gate convention (prompt-index, public-types, env-vars)' with 'using a pytest unit test (a different but complementary mechanism to the repo's CI shell-based drift gates for prompt-index, public-types, and env-vars)' — or explain the positive rationale for choosing pytest over the shell-script pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the inaccurate 'regenerate-and-diff' analogy with a precise description: the new `test_skill_shared_sync.py` is a static file-content equality test auto-collected by `pytest` (no CI YAML changes required), analogous to the style of `test_version_sync.py` or `test_reuse_surface_doc.py` rather than the script-driven prompt-index/env-vars/public-types gates.
findings.finding: The plan characterises the new drift-gate unit test as 'mirroring the repo's regenerate-and-diff drift-gate convention (prompt-index, public-types, env-vars)', but this analogy is inaccurate. Inspecting `tests/unit/test_ci_workflow_parity.py` shows that 'public-types' maps to the `gen_types` CI script and 'env-vars' maps to `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` — both are workflow-YAML-driven script gates enforced by `test_ci_workflow_parity.py`, not pytest byte-identity tests. Only prompt-index has a pytest companion (`test_prompt_index.py`), which regenerates a JSON index and compares it — structurally different from a static file-copy comparison. The proposed `test_skill_shared_sync.py` is actually a pure pytest file-content equality test, which needs no CI YAML changes (it is auto-collected by `pytest -m 'not integration and not external'`). An implementer following the stated analogy literally could either (a) add unnecessary CI workflow YAML entries that then break `test_ci_workflow_parity.py`'s asymmetry guard, or (b) believe unneeded steps are required, introducing confusion about the implementation scope.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Reference mechanism' section: 'it runs under `make test`, an existing gating CI command, mirroring the repo's regenerate-and-diff drift-gate convention (prompt-index, public-types, env-vars)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'mirroring the repo's regenerate-and-diff drift-gate convention (prompt-index, public-types, env-vars)' to 'a static file-content equality test auto-collected under the existing `pytest -m "not integration and not external"` suite — no CI workflow YAML changes required; analogous to `test_version_sync.py`'s pin-and-assert pattern'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a prominent comment in `tests/unit/test_skill_shared_sync.py` beside the hardcoded consumer list stating 'UPDATE THIS LIST when adding a new consuming skill' (and consider adding the same instruction to the README one-liner block), so the omission is at least self-documenting at the enforcement point rather than only in the README prose.
findings.finding: The drift-gate test hardcodes the three-skill consumer set with no auto-detection or enforcement mechanism. If a future skill is added that should consume test-design.md, a developer must manually update both the test AND the sync one-liner. Forgetting to update the test means the drift gate silently skips the new skill, providing false confidence.
findings.location: tests/unit/test_skill_shared_sync.py (described in 'Reference mechanism' section and AC item 3); README sync one-liner
findings.suggested_fix: In the test file, define the consumer list as a named constant with an explanatory comment: `# Hardcoded by design: rebar-janitor is excluded (it never authors tests). ADD new consumers here AND update the README one-liner.\nCONSUMING_SKILLS = ['rebar-debug', 'rebar-implement', 'rebar-brainstorm']`. Also add the same reminder to the README one-liner block so both update points are co-located with their rationale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC proof that `make test` actually invokes `tests/unit/test_skill_shared_sync.py` (e.g. `make test --collect-only | grep test_skill_shared_sync` or confirm the Makefile/pytest config includes all of tests/unit/).
findings.finding: The plan states the new drift test 'runs under `make test`, an existing gating CI command' but does not verify that `tests/unit/` is actually included in `make test`. If `make test` scopes to a subset of tests/unit or uses a marker/filter that would exclude a new file, the gating claim could be false.
findings.location: Plan body: 'runs under `make test`, an existing gating CI command, mirroring the repo's regenerate-and-diff drift-gate convention'; AC bullet 14: '`make lint` and `.venv/bin/pytest tests/unit/test_skill_shared_sync.py -q` pass'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the plan (or README update section) that `examples/claude-skills/shared/` is a new directory to create, so the implementation step is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The `shared/` directory does not currently exist under `examples/claude-skills/`. The plan assumes `examples/claude-skills/shared/test-design.md` can be created by simply writing the file, but if the tooling or CI enforces that only known subdirectories are valid (e.g. a directory listing test), the new `shared/` dir creation should be explicitly called out as a required step rather than implicitly assumed.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the 'What' section: 'Create the `examples/claude-skills/shared/` directory and within it `test-design.md` as the canonical file.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the plan's rebar-debug integration section, specify the exact replacement wording (or a binding template) for the `llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md` Phase 2 RED definition, so the implementer is not required to invent it.
findings.finding: The plan updates both rebar-debug/SKILL.md Step 4's LLM-surface paragraph AND llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md's Phase 2 bullet to cite `test-design.md`'s quantified-eval standard, but does not specify the authority chain between these two references or the replacement wording for the taxonomy bullet — leaving an implementer to invent both.
findings.location: Plan 'What' section — rebar-debug integration: 'Step 4's existing LLM-surface paragraph is updated…' and 'llm-behavioral-taxonomy.md's Phase 2 (Repair) bullet — whose current wording defines RED as a single failing eval/behavioural assertion — is updated to cite test-design.md's quantified-eval requirements'

findings.finding: AC 5 and AC 6 use `grep -n -A3 <anchor>` (3-line context window) to prove that `test-design.md` appears in the LLM-surface paragraph (AC 5) and that the handoff list contains the contract card (AC 6). If `test-design.md` appears more than 3 lines after the anchor string, or the handoff list spans more than 3 lines, the grep exits 0 without the citation ever appearing in its output — silently confirming a criterion that was never satisfied.
findings.location: AC lines: 'rebar-debug Step 4's LLM-surface paragraph defers to the shared eval standard' (AC 5) and 'rebar-debug Step 5 hands the fixer the contract card' (AC 6)
findings.suggested_fix:
AC 5: `awk '/^## Step 4/,/^## Step [56]/' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/SKILL.md | grep -q 'test-design.md'`
AC 6: `awk '/Hand fix subagent only/,/^##/' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/SKILL.md | grep -q 'contract card'`

findings.finding: AC 11 (README documents the convention) uses an OR-pattern grep (`grep -n 'test-design|byte-identical|cp examples/claude-skills/shared'`) but the prose criterion requires BOTH 'the canonical-file note AND the sync one-liner'. A README that mentions only 'byte-identical' — but omits the sync one-liner — satisfies the grep while failing the actual requirement.
findings.location: AC: 'examples/claude-skills/README.md documents the convention. Prove: `grep -n 'test-design|byte-identical|cp examples/claude-skills/shared' examples/claude-skills/README.md` shows the canonical-file note and the sync one-liner.'

findings.finding: The plan names exactly three consuming skills (rebar-debug, rebar-implement, rebar-brainstorm) and hardcodes them in the sync one-liner, but rebar-janitor/ exists as a fourth skill directory alongside them. The AC for test_skill_shared_sync.py says it 'asserts byte-identity of every copy' — if the test auto-discovers all skill directories under examples/claude-skills/ it will fail on rebar-janitor (no copy planned); if it hardcodes only the three named skills, rebar-janitor will silently bypass the gate forever. The plan doesn't specify the test's discovery scope and doesn't address whether rebar-janitor should receive a copy or be explicitly excluded.
findings.location: AC item 3 (test_skill_shared_sync.py) and 'Reference mechanism' section (sync one-liner)
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (a) add rebar-janitor to the sync one-liner and copy list (if it consumes the standard), or (b) have the test discover skills dynamically as directories containing a SKILL.md and skip non-consuming skills via an explicit exclusion set (e.g. _NON_CONSUMING = {'rebar-janitor'}), documented in the test with a reason. Update the AC to match whichever scope is chosen.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Sharpen the rebar-brainstorm trigger from the vague 'will include or reference testing, verification, or behavioral oracles' to a concrete positive indicator (e.g. 'whenever the AC names an expected behavior, a pass/fail outcome, a test, or an oracle'), or replace the conditional entirely with an unconditional 'before recording any ticket, read the shared file' (matching the plan's stated 'when in doubt, read it' intent).
findings.finding: The rebar-brainstorm integration adds a trigger condition — 'before recording any ticket whose acceptance criteria will include or reference testing, verification, or behavioral oracles' — that requires the LLM agent to self-evaluate a vague predicate at instruction-execution time, with no concrete heuristic co-located at the point of the instruction.
findings.location: Plan 'What' section — rebar-brainstorm/SKILL.md integration paragraph; rebar-brainstorm/SKILL.md 'Before recording tickets' section
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the conditional with an unconditional instruction: 'Before recording any ticket, read `test-design.md` in this skill's directory — the cost is one file read and it ensures every AC is correctly shaped.' This eliminates the vague predicate entirely and aligns with the plan's own 'when in doubt, read it (the cost is one file read)' rationale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale in the 'Reference mechanism' paragraph explaining why a separate shared file is chosen over inline expansion in each SKILL.md (e.g., single point of update, no three-way drift, extensible to future skills).
findings.finding: The plan gives a positive rationale for choosing real-file copies over symlinks, but never states a positive rationale for the more fundamental architectural choice: a separate shared companion file (`test-design.md`) rather than embedding the test-design content inline in each SKILL.md. The 'why a separate file?' question — maintainability, single point of update, future-skill extensibility — is entirely absent, leaving the central design choice asserted rather than argued.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Reference mechanism' paragraph: 'shared/test-design.md is the canonical copy; each consuming skill directory ships a REAL FILE…'
findings.suggested_fix: Insert after the first sentence of the 'Reference mechanism' paragraph: 'A single source is preferred over duplicating the prose inline in three SKILL.md files because a future update or correction requires exactly one edit rather than three coordinated ones, and the pattern extends cleanly to future skills without per-skill authoring.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-line fallback instruction in each consuming SKILL.md section that references `test-design.md` (e.g. 'if the file is not present, apply the oracle-design principles stated inline below as a condensed form') or explicitly state that absence is a hard stop with a user-facing message.
findings.finding: No SKILL.md specifies a fallback when `test-design.md` is absent at skill-execution time. The plan adds `test-design.md` as a required companion file across three skills but gives no graceful-degradation instruction — an LLM agent executing the skill in a context where only SKILL.md was copied will encounter a broken reference with no guidance on how to proceed.
findings.location: Plan 'What' section — Reference mechanism; rebar-debug/SKILL.md Step 4 integration; rebar-implement/SKILL.md Phase 4a integration; rebar-brainstorm/SKILL.md integration

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
